text: Chapter 1 establishes the foundational vocabulary and conceptual framework for jazz soloing, defining single-note soloing as playing successions of single notes that form melodies, and organizing instruction around three core questions: what notes sound good over chord progressions, where those notes are on the instrument, and how to play them interestingly. The chapter introduces the major scale as the reference system from which all other scales derive, and teaches the atomic building blocks—half-step and whole-step intervals—along with a numeric notation system (scale degrees 1-7) for describing both scales and chords. The master's characteristic move is the chord formula system, which expresses any chord as a numeric pattern derived from the major scale (e.g., major 7th = 1, 3, 5, 7; minor 7th = 1, b3, 5, b7), extended to upper partials (9ths, 11ths, 13ths), and paired with appropriate scales via the operational concept that a chord "takes" a scale when that scale sounds good over it. The chapter concludes with the first concrete chord-scale rule: major Group 1 chords typically take either the major or Lydian scale (the major scale with a raised 4th), establishing a pattern the method will build upon throughout.
